main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaves, cinnamon)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Lamb Rogan Josh is approximately 300 g, comprising braised lamb pieces in a rich spiced gravy, usually served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton curry (with gravy) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not list Rogan Josh by name. The closest approved match is 'Mutton curry (with gravy)' from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition). Rogan Josh is a Kashmiri braised lamb curry with a comparable ingredient profile (lamb, oil/ghee, onion, yogurt, spices). Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mutton curry entry. Sodium is estimated upward slightly to reflect the restaurant preparation context. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked' (NDB 17003) was used as a cross-reference for protein and fat plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Rogan Josh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 25 (low) based on the analogous food category of meat-based curry dishes with minimal carbohydrate content. Meat dishes with low carbohydrate content consistently fall in the low GI range (GI < 30) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The carbohydrate content of Rogan Josh is approximately 3.5 g per 100 g, derived primarily from onions and yogurt, both of which are low-GI ingredients. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (25 × 10.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.6 g, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Lamb Rogan Josh provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing 49–57% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 25, Lamb Rogan Josh has a very low glycemic load of approximately 2.6 per standard serving, making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided the accompanying rice or bread portion is controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb Rogan Josh contains approximately 4.2 g saturated fat and 380 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g, choose lean lamb cuts, and request preparation with minimal ghee to reduce saturated fat intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Rogan Josh is a classic slow-braised lamb curry originating from the Kashmir Valley of northern India, and is one of the signature dishes of Kashmiri Wazwan cuisine. The dish is prepared by browning bone-in or boneless lamb pieces in ghee or oil, then slow-cooking them in a deeply spiced gravy built from Kashmiri red chillies (which impart a vivid red colour without excessive heat), dried ginger powder (sonth), fennel powder (saunf), whole spices including cardamom, cloves, cinnamon and bay leaves, and yogurt. Asafoetida (hing) is a traditional flavouring agent in the authentic Kashmiri Pandit version, which does not use onions or garlic. Restaurant versions served in Singapore and internationally typically include onions and garlic in the base. The resulting dish has a rich, aromatic, mildly spiced gravy with tender, fall-off-the-bone lamb. The flavour profile is warm, earthy, and mildly tangy from the yogurt. Nutritionally, Rogan Josh is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content (approximately 14 g protein, 11 g fat, and 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The estimated glycemic index is 25 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 300 g serving is approximately 2.6, which is clinically negligible. The primary nutritional concerns are sodium content in restaurant preparations and saturated fat from lamb and ghee, which are relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Lamb Rogan Josh is naturally gluten-free and is halal when prepared with halal-certified lamb.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in Lamb Rogan Josh?
Is Rogan Josh good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Rogan Josh and butter chicken?
What spices are in Rogan Josh?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Lamb Rogan Josh exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN 'Mutton curry (with gravy)' entry, which is the closest approved analogue. GI is estimated from analogous low-carbohydrate meat curry data per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Mutton curry (with gravy)' as no direct Rogan Josh entry exists in approved sources. Sodium value (380 mg/100 g) is estimated upward from the ICMR-NIN base value to reflect typical restaurant preparation with added salt. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are cross-referenced from USDA SR Legacy lamb composite data and are approximate. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Rogan Josh was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as (25 × 10.5) / 100 = 2.6. The input food_name 'Lamb Rogan Josh (Chef Recommendation)' has been normalised to the canonical English name 'Lamb Rogan Josh'; the parenthetical marketing descriptor has been removed. Halal status is assumed based on the dish's Islamic culinary tradition; individual restaurant certification should be verified. The dish is served at three field survey data restaurants: Mirch Masalah (Boat Quay), Khushboo – The Fragrance of India (Boat Quay), and Curry Village – Authentic North Indian Cuisine.
